data.title: Anglo American PLC Shares Up 3% After Production Report
European indices open higher to kick start the day
data.description:
Key points:
  • Eurostoxx +0.4%
  • Germany DAX +0.8%
  • France CAC 40 +0.3%
  • UK FTSE +0.6%
  • Spain IBEX +0.5%
  • Italy FTSE MIB +0.3%

Risk is back on, at least for now, as European stocks look to catch up to the gains on Wall Street overnight. US futures are also higher but only marginally, up 0.1% currently. As tariff fears subside, the focus turns back to economic data with the weekly jobless claims report coming up later before the non-farm payrolls tomorrow.

data.title: Anglo Platinum profit set to almost halve on weak prices, restructuring costs
data.description:
Anglo American Platinum expects headline earnings for the 2024 financial year to drop by as much as 46% on the back of persistently low metals prices and costs associated with restructuring and a demerger from its parent company.

The world's top producer of platinum group metals used to curb emissions in motor vehicles said it expects headline earnings between 7.6 billion rand and 9 billion rand ($408.8 million-$484.1 million) in the year ended December 31, down from 14 billion rand in 2023.

Amplats said dollar prices of palladium and rhodium fell by 24% and 30%, respectively, in the past year.

The platinum miner is set to be separated from the Anglo American group by mid-year, its CEO Craig Miller told Reuters on Wednesday.

The spinoff is part of the group's restructuring strategy, aimed at fighting off a $49 billion takeover bid from bigger rival BHP to focus on more profitable copper and iron ore assets.

Amplats said it had incurred 3.5 billion rand in costs associated with operational and corporate restructurings, including the demerger.

($1 = 18.5896 rand)

data.title: Hang Seng Tech Index Closes 2.6% Higher at 5059.38
Hang Seng Index Closes 1.4% Higher at 20891.62
Zhejiang Zhongxin Environmental Protection Technology Group
data.description:
Zhejiang Zhongxin Environmental Protection Technology Group started production of its first workshop at a factory in Thailand's Golden Pond Industrial Park after completing trial production.

The trial confirmed the advanced performance of the company's independently developed production equipment, surpassing current domestic standards, according to a Thursday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The factory is expected to produce more than 30,000 tons of pulp-molded tableware in 2025, with ramp-up to full capacity of 50,000 tons by 2026.

Shares of the pulp tableware producer closed 1% higher Thursday.

data.title: OUE Healthcare Joint Venture Enters Strategic Partnership with Zhongda Hospital Southeast University
data.description:
OUE Healthcare , through its joint venture hospital, Changshu China Merchants - Lippo Obstetrics & Gynaecology Hospital, entered into a strategic partnership with Zhongda Hospital Southeast University, according to a company release on Wednesday.

Under the partnership, the two entities will establish a two-way 'green channel' for patient access to develop advanced clinica services.

The partnership will also focus on research, education, nursing and management.

Shares of the healthcare group were up nearly 4% in recent trading.

data.title: Volvo Car Shares Fall 6.2% After 4Q Earnings
Telenor Shares Rise 0.6% After 4Q Earnings
Sterling Falls Ahead of BOE Rate Decision — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling falls as investors exercise caution ahead of the Bank of England's interest-rate decision at 1200 GMT. The market is pricing in a 94% chance of the BOE cutting rates by 25 basis points, according to LSEG. This marks the first decision since the U.K. government bond selloff last month. "Indeed, at the height of the pound's slump, there had been speculation about whether the BOE would cut at all at this meeting," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. However, the latest inflation data were lower than expected and this supported market bets for another cut. Deutsche Bank expects a 8-1 vote in favor of a cut. GBP/USD falls 0.2% to 1.2476. EUR/GBP rises 0.1% to 0.8325. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: A.P. Moller-Maersk Swings to Profit as Red Sea Disruptions Boost Freight Rates — Update
data.description:
By Pierre Bertrand

A.P. Moeller-Maersk swung to a better-than-expected net profit in the fourth quarter as disruptions to the Red Sea shipping supported higher shipping rates.

The Danish shipping company said it made $2.085 billion in net profit for the quarter, compared with a net loss of $436 million a year ago, when an oversupply of cargo capacity weighed on freight rates.

Quarterly revenue came to $14.59 billion, a 24% on-year increase as continued disruption to shipping lanes in the Red Sea pushed up shipping rates by 38% in the quarter.

The result compares with analysts' expectations of $1.31 billion in net profit on $13.89 billion in revenue, according to a Visible Alpha consensus.

Maersk's ocean segment, its container shipping business and the company's largest division, posted a 39% increase to revenue in the reporting period.

The company said it would propose a 1,120 Danish kroner dividend after separately announcing Wednesday evening the start of a share buyback program of up to around $2 billion.

The company said that for 2025 it is targeting $6 billion to $9 billion in underlying earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, up to $3 billion in underlying earnings before interest and taxes.

Write to Pierre Bertrand at pierre.bertrand@wsj.com

data.title: RailTel Corporation Bags New Contract Worth INR497 Million
data.description:
RailTel Corporation of India has secured a new order worth 496.8 million Indian rupees from the North Frontier Railway, India, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The contract entails the provision of video surveillance system at 340 stations of the North Frontier Railway. It is to be executed by May 5, 2026.

data.title: Software Companies More Insulated From Tariffs Than Hardware Firms — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. tariffs will likely have a minimal impact on software companies, while hardware companies will face a bigger hit, Janus Henderson Investors says in a commentary. Software isn't reliant on physical asset production and has a geographical distributed workforce, whereas hardware is often manufactured abroad and relies on components such as semiconductor chips and wafers. The components are mainly produced in Asia-Pacific and then shipped to Mexico for assembly to take advantage of lower labor costs, the asset manager adds. "The impact of the recently announced tariffs would therefore be most pronounced in the assembly phase." Janus Henderson expects the impact on large end-market manufacturers like Dell and HP to be more acute than their suppliers. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

data.title: Orsted Shares Rise 6.5% After 4Q Earnings
GlobalData to Launch $62.5 Million Buyback; Plans to List on London's Main Market
data.description:
By Anthony O. Goriainoff

GlobalData said it would launch a 50 million-pound ($62.5 million) buyback program and that it intends to move its listing to the London Stock Exchange's main market from the AIM.

The data, analytics and insights platform said Thursday that admission to the main market would improve its profile and allow a broader group of U.K. and global institutional shareholders to own its ordinary shares.

GlobalData also said that it has agreed to extend Chairman Murray Legg's term for up to three years to help with orderly succession planning for the board.

Write to Anthony O. Goriainoff at anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com

data.title: Trade War Between U.S. And China Could Weaken Eurozone Economy, ECB's Cipollone Says — Update
data.description:
By Paul Hannon

A trade war between the U.S. and China could lead to weaker economic growth and falling inflation in the eurozone, a top official at the European Central Bank warned in an interview published Thursday.

Speaking to Reuters, ECB Executive Board member Piero Cipollone said high U.S. tariffs on imports from the world's second largest economy could push Chinese businesses to look for buyers elsewhere, and increase their competition with eurozone rivals.

"Trade barriers will force China to sell its goods elsewhere and the competition from China could be a serious threat to us," he said. "These goods showing up in Europe could have both a deflationary and a contractionary impact because they would crowd out local products."

The U.S. and China entered a new trade war on Tuesday, with tariffs of 10% going into effect on Chinese goods. Beijing struck back with 10%-15% levies that are set to take effect on Feb. 10 on select American goods.

The ECB last week lowered its key interest for a fifth time since June 2024, and investors expect further cuts in upcoming meetings of its governing council as inflation cools and economic growth remains weak.

However, Cipollone said that given the high levels of uncertainty about the economic outlook that partly derives from U.S. trade policy, "anything can happen."

"We all agree there is still room for adjusting rates downwards," he said. "But we need to be extremely careful."

U.S. President Trump has made it clear that U.S. allies across the Atlantic are high on his target list for tariffs. But Cipollone said the imbalances in trade between the two economic giants are smaller than they appear.

"It's true we have a goods surplus with the United States," he said. "But if you add in services and look at the overall current account, then the balance is close to zero."

ECB rate setters have become more concerned about the outlook for growth in the eurozone economy, which stagnated in the final quarter of last year.

Cipollone said that disappointing outcome might be a reflection of uncertainty about the outlook for the global economy in the run-up to the U.S. presidential election and its aftermath.

"The real problem is investments, and that is only partially linked to monetary policy," he said. "The culprit is uncertainty. Investments have been weak since the summer given the overall uncertainty and the direction of trade policy after the US election."

Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com

data.title: Ma'aden Kicks Off US Dollar-denominated Trust Certificates Issue
data.description:
Saudi Arabian Mining (SASE:1211), d/b/a Ma'aden, launched the offer for its US dollar-denominated trust certificates via a special purpose vehicle to qualified investors on Thursday, according to same-day release.

The Saudi Arabian mining group's offer, which will end on Friday, will have a par value and a minimum subscription amount of $200,000. The company said the sukuk's price and return are subject to market conditions.

The company tapped Citigroup Global Markets, HSBC Bank, Al Rajhi Capital, BNP Paribas, GIB Capital, J.P. Morgan Securities, Natixis, Saudi Fransi Capital, SNB Capital and Standard Chartered Bank as joint lead managers for the offer.

data.title: Watches of Switzerland Group's Performance in Line With Expectations
data.description:
By Najat Kantouar

Watches of Switzerland Group said its third quarter was in line with expectations, and that it was on track to deliver on its fiscal 2025 guidance.

The luxury-watch retailer said Thursday that its performance for the 13 weeks to 26 January was in line with its expectations, supported by good holiday periods in both the U.K. and U.S.

Demand for its key luxury brands remained strong in the period, outstripping supply in both the U.K. and U.S. markets, it added.

Meanwhile, the performances of businesses it already owned in North America and the recent acquisition of jewelry designer and manufacturer Roberto Coin performances in were encouraging. Over the period, the U.K. markets for both luxury watches and jewelry stabilized, while the U.S. market has seen continued momentum.

The London-based luxury watch retailer continues to forecast revenue in the 1.67 billion pound to 1.73 billion pound range ($2.09 billion-$2.16 billion), with growth between 9% and 12% at constant currency. It also projects an expansion in the adjusted earnings before interest and taxes margin from 20 basis points in fiscal 2024, which ended on April 28, to 60 basis points in fiscal 2025.

The group expects to complete key projects in the fourth quarter and will open a new flagship Rolex boutique in Old Bond Street, London in March.

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

Macquarie Starts Yunnan Aluminium at Outperform with 18.40 Yuan Price Target
Orsted Expects Earnings to Rise This Year as It Works to Shore Up Balance Sheet
data.description:
(07:04 GMT) *Orsted Sees 2025 EBITDA Excluding New Partnership Agreements and Cancellation Fees at DKK25B-DKK28B
(07:04 GMT) *Orsted Maintains Target to Reinstate Dividends From Financial Year 2026
(07:05 GMT) *Orsted: Partnership and Divestment Program is On Track
(07:05 GMT) *Orsted: 2025 Gross Investments Expected to be DKK50B-DKK54B
(07:08 GMT) *Orsted 4Q Rev DKK21.08B
(07:08 GMT) *Orsted: Analysts Saw 4Q Revenue At DKK26.02B
(07:08 GMT) *Orsted 4Q EBITDA DKK8.35B
(07:08 GMT) *Orsted: Analysts Saw 4Q Ebitda At DKK8.65B
(07:09 GMT) *Orsted 4Q Net Loss DKK6.08B
(07:09 GMT) *Orsted: Analysts Saw 4Q Net Profit At DKK2.74B
(07:13 GMT) *Orsted Sees Higher Offshore and Onshore Earnings in 2025
(07:15 GMT) *Orsted Sees Significantly Higher Bioenergy & Other Earnings in 2025
(07:15 GMT) *Orsted: 2025 Gross Investments Seen at DKK50B-DKK54B
(07:58 GMT) Orsted Expects Earnings to Rise This Year as It Works to Shore Up Balance Sheet

By Dominic Chopping

Orsted guided for slightly higher earnings this year as some wind and solar farms ramp up and it expects to receive higher payments from wind-farm contracts to account for inflation.

The Danish renewable-energy company expects earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization excluding new partnerships and cancellation fees at between 25 billion and 28 billion kroner ($3.49 billion-$3.9 billion), up from 24.75 billion kroner in 2024.

The company announced late Wednesday that it will cut its investment program by 25% in the period to 2030 to ensure a stronger balance sheet and support a solid investment grade rating.

The news comes days after the company parted ways with Chief Executive Officer Mads Nipper, who has been replaced by Rasmus Errboe, its former deputy chief executive and chief commercial officer.

On Thursday, Orsted reported fourth-quarter Ebitda excluding new partnerships and cancellation fees--the company's preferred metric--of 7.55 billion kroner, down from 8.62 billion kroner a year prior.

The net loss widened to 6.08 billion kroner from a loss of 284 million kroner, as revenue fell 2.1% to 21.08 billion kroner.

A FactSet consensus had seen net profit at 2.74 billion kroner on revenue of 26.02 billion kroner.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com
